output:
# Compound Agent Active

> **Context Recovery**: Run `npx ca prime` after compaction, clear, or new session

## CLI Commands (ALWAYS USE THESE)

**You MUST use CLI commands for lesson management:**

| Command | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `npx ca search "query"` | Search lessons - MUST call before architectural decisions; use anytime you need context |
| `npx ca learn "insight"` | Capture lessons - call AFTER corrections or discoveries |

## Core Constraints

**Default**: Use CLI commands for lesson management
**Prohibited**: NEVER edit .claude/lessons/ files directly

**Default**: Propose lessons freely after corrections
**Prohibited**: NEVER propose without quality gate (novel + specific; prefer actionable)

## Retrieval Protocol

You MUST call `npx ca search` BEFORE:
- Architectural decisions or complex planning
- Implementing patterns you've done before in this repo

**NEVER skip search for complex decisions.** Past mistakes will repeat.

Beyond mandatory triggers, use these commands freely — they are lightweight queries, not heavyweight operations. Uncertain about a pattern? `ca search`. The cost of an unnecessary search is near-zero; the cost of a missed one can be hours.

## Capture Protocol

Run `npx ca learn` AFTER:
- User corrects you ("no", "wrong", "actually...")
- You self-correct after iteration failures
- Test fails then you fix it

**Quality gate** (must pass before capturing):
- Novel (not already stored)
- Specific (clear guidance)
- Actionable (preferred, not mandatory)

**Workflow**: Search BEFORE deciding, capture AFTER learning.

# Beads Workflow Context

> **Context Recovery**: Run `bd prime` after compaction, clear, or new session
> Hooks auto-call this in Claude Code when .beads/ detected

# 🚨 SESSION CLOSE PROTOCOL 🚨

**CRITICAL**: Before saying "done" or "complete", you MUST run this checklist:

```
[ ] 1. git status              (check what changed)
[ ] 2. git add <files>         (stage code changes)
[ ] 3. git commit -m "..."     (commit code)
[ ] 4. git push                (push to remote)
```

**NEVER skip this.** Work is not done until pushed.

## Core Rules
- **Default**: Use beads for ALL task tracking (`bd create`, `bd ready`, `bd close`)
- **Prohibited**: Do NOT use TodoWrite, TaskCreate, or markdown files for task tracking
- **Workflow**: Create beads issue BEFORE writing code, mark in_progress when starting
- **Memory**: Use `bd remember "insight"` for persistent knowledge across sessions. Do NOT use MEMORY.md files — they fragment across accounts. Search with `bd memories <keyword>`.
- Persistence you don't need beats lost context
- Git workflow: beads auto-commit to Dolt, run `git push` at session end
- Session management: check `bd ready` for available work

## Essential Commands

### Finding Work
- `bd ready` - Show issues ready to work (no blockers)
- `bd list --status=open` - All open issues
- `bd list --status=in_progress` - Your active work
- `bd show <id>` - Detailed issue view with dependencies

### Creating & Updating
- `bd create --title="Summary of this issue" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task|bug|feature --priority=2` - New issue
  - Priority: 0-4 or P0-P4 (0=critical, 2=medium, 4=backlog). NOT "high"/"medium"/"low"
- `bd update <id> --claim` - Claim work
- `bd update <id> --assignee=username` - Assign to someone
- `bd update <id> --title/--description/--notes/--design` - Update fields inline
- `bd close <id>` - Mark complete
- `bd close <id1> <id2> ...` - Close multiple issues at once (more efficient)
- `bd close <id> --reason="explanation"` - Close with reason
- **Tip**: When creating multiple issues/tasks/epics, use parallel subagents for efficiency
- **WARNING**: Do NOT use `bd edit` - it opens $EDITOR (vim/nano) which blocks agents

### Dependencies & Blocking
- `bd dep add <issue> <depends-on>` - Add dependency (issue depends on depends-on)
- `bd blocked` - Show all blocked issues
- `bd show <id>` - See what's blocking/blocked by this issue

### Sync & Collaboration
- `bd dolt push` - Push beads to Dolt remote
- `bd dolt pull` - Pull beads from Dolt remote
- `bd search <query>` - Search issues by keyword

### Project Health
- `bd stats` - Project statistics (open/closed/blocked counts)
- `bd doctor` - Check for issues (sync problems, missing hooks)

## Common Workflows

**Starting work:**
```bash
bd ready           # Find available work
bd show <id>       # Review issue details
bd update <id> --claim  # Claim it
```

**Completing work:**
```bash
bd close <id1> <id2> ...    # Close all completed issues at once
git add . && git commit -m "..."  # Commit code changes
git push                    # Push to remote
```

**Creating dependent work:**
```bash
# Run bd create commands in parallel (use subagents for many items)
bd create --title="Implement feature X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=feature
bd create --title="Write tests for X" --description="Why this issue exists and what needs to be done" --type=task
bd dep add beads-yyy beads-xxx  # Tests depend on Feature (Feature blocks tests)
```

content.content:
○ long-running-harness-13h [EPIC] · E9: Synthesizer Engine   [● P1 · OPEN]
Owner: Nathan Delacrétaz · Type: epic
Created: 2026-03-28 · Updated: 2026-03-28

DESCRIPTION
2-oscillator architecture with PolyBLEP antialiasing in AudioWorklet (saw, square, triangle, sine). Per-oscillator: type selector, octave, detune, mix level. BiquadFilterNode for main filter (LP/HP/BP) with cutoff and resonance. ADSR amplitude envelope (exponential curves). ADSR filter envelope with depth control. 2 LFOs with rate, depth, shape (sine/square/triangle/S&H). Hardwired modulation paths: LFO1->pitch, LFO1->cutoff, LFO2->amplitude, filter env->cutoff, velocity->filter, velocity->amplitude. Voice allocator: 16-voice pool (fixed-size, INV-5) with oldest-steal + 5ms crossfade (MIT-H4-5). Minimum release time floor of 2ms (MIT-H4-6). Legato mode with pitch glide (R-STA-05). Instrument track type: receives MIDI events, routes audio to mixer channel. Virtual keyboard UI for mouse-triggered notes. Synth editor panel: oscillator type selectors, filter cutoff/resonance knobs, ADSR sliders, LFO rate/depth knobs. Real-time parameter changes via AudioParam (R-EVT-08). Zero-allocation process() (INV-3, NFR-14). EARS: R-EVT-04, R-EVT-08, R-STA-05, R-UNW-07, R-UBI-02, NFR-01, NFR-03, NFR-14. Interface exports: SynthInstrument, SynthParameterMap, SynthVoiceCommand. QA: FFT pitch verification, envelope shape tests, voice-stealing audibility, zero-allocation compliance. Research: docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md

DEPENDS ON
  → ✓ long-running-harness-5he: E2: Neo-Brutalist Design System ● P1
  → ✓ long-running-harness-9sl: E1: Project Shell & Audio Infrastructure ● P0
  → ✓ long-running-harness-bqt: E7: Mixer Core ● P0

BLOCKS
  ← ○ long-running-harness-70a: (EPIC) E15: Arpeggiator ● P2
  ← ○ long-running-harness-9yc: (EPIC) E12: Modulation Matrix ● P2
  ← ○ long-running-harness-aow: (EPIC) E17: Bounce/Export ● P1
  ← ○ long-running-harness-nl5: (EPIC) E11: Piano Roll & MIDI Editor ● P1
  ← ○ long-running-harness-tnf: (EPIC) E18: Integration Verification ● P1

content.content:
1	# Agent Instructions
2	
3	This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
4	
5	## Project Map
6	
7	```
8	README.md             # Project overview
9	AGENTS.md             # YOU ARE HERE -- agent entry point
10	index.html            # Vite entry point
11	package.json          # Dependencies and scripts
12	src/
13	  main.tsx            # React entry point
14	  App.tsx             # Root component
15	  audio/              # Web Audio API engine (path alias: @audio)
16	  ui/                 # UI components (path alias: @ui)
17	  state/              # State management (path alias: @state)
18	  test/               # Test setup and utilities
19	docs/
20	  INDEX.md            # Documentation hub
21	  adr/                # Architectural Decision Records
22	  research/           # Deep research papers
23	  compound/           # Compound-agent documentation
24	.claude/
25	  CLAUDE.md           # Project instructions + agentic principles
26	  skills/             # Skill definitions (compound workflows)
27	  agents/             # Subagent definitions
28	  commands/           # Slash command definitions
29	  lessons/            # Session memory (NEVER edit directly -- use `ca` CLI)
30	.beads/               # Issue tracking data (Dolt-powered)
31	```
32	
33	## Stack
34	
35	- **React 19 + TypeScript 5.8** (strict mode, `noUncheckedIndexedAccess`, `exactOptionalPropertyTypes`)
36	- **Vite 8** for dev server and build
37	- **Vitest 4** for testing (jsdom environment, @testing-library/react)
38	- **ESLint 10** with typescript-eslint strict type-checked rules
39	- **Prettier** for formatting
40	- **Husky** pre-commit hooks (lint + format check)
41	- **GitHub Actions CI** (lint, format, test, build)
42	
43	## Build Commands
44	
45	```bash
46	pnpm dev              # Start dev server
47	pnpm build            # Type-check + production build
48	pnpm test             # Run tests once
49	pnpm test:watch       # Run tests in watch mode
50	pnpm test:coverage    # Run tests with coverage
51	pnpm lint             # ESLint + TypeScript noEmit
52	pnpm lint:fix         # Auto-fix lint issues
53	pnpm format           # Format source files
54	pnpm format:check     # Check formatting (CI)
55	pnpm check            # Full quality gate: lint + format + test
56	```
57	
58	## Path Aliases
59	
60	Use `@/`, `@audio/`, `@ui/`, `@state/` instead of deep relative imports. ESLint enforces this (no `../../../` allowed).
61	
62	## Conventions
63	
64	- **Issue tracking**: `bd` only. No markdown TODOs.
65	- **Session memory**: `ca search` before decisions, `ca learn` after discoveries.
66	- **Decisions**: Record in `docs/adr/` using the template. See [ADR-0001](docs/adr/0001-initial-tooling-choices.md).
67	- **Research**: Extensive docs in `docs/research/`. Send subagents there for context (`ca knowledge "topic"`).
68	- **Tests**: Write tests first (TDD). Tests live next to source files as `*.test.tsx`/`*.test.ts`.
69	- **Types**: Strict TS everywhere. No `any`. Explicit return types on functions.
70	
71	## Quick Reference
72	
73	```bash
74	bd ready              # Find available work
75	bd show <id>          # View issue details
76	bd update <id> --claim  # Claim work atomically
77	bd close <id>         # Complete work
78	bd dolt push          # Push beads data to remote
79	```
80	
81	## Non-Interactive Shell Commands
82	
83	**ALWAYS use non-interactive flags** with file operations to avoid hanging on confirmation prompts.
84	
85	Shell commands like `cp`, `mv`, and `rm` may be aliased to include `-i` (interactive) mode on some systems, causing the agent to hang indefinitely waiting for y/n input.
86	
87	**Use these forms instead:**
88	```bash
89	# Force overwrite without prompting
90	cp -f source dest           # NOT: cp source dest
91	mv -f source dest           # NOT: mv source dest
92	rm -f file                  # NOT: rm file
93	
94	# For recursive operations
95	rm -rf directory            # NOT: rm -r directory
96	cp -rf source dest          # NOT: cp -r source dest
97	```
98	
99	**Other commands that may prompt:**
100	- `scp` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` for non-interactive
101	- `ssh` - use `-o BatchMode=yes` to fail instead of prompting
102	- `apt-get` - use `-y` flag
103	- `brew` - use `HOMEBREW_NO_AUTO_UPDATE=1` env var
104	
105	<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION profile:full hash:d4f96305 -->
106	## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
107	
108	**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
109	
110	### Why bd?
111	
112	- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
113	- Git-friendly: Dolt-powered version control with native sync
114	- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
115	- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
116	
117	### Quick Start
118	
119	**Check for ready work:**
120	
121	```bash
122	bd ready --json
123	```
124	
125	**Create new issues:**
126	
127	```bash
128	bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
129	bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
130	```
131	
132	**Claim and update:**
133	
134	```bash
135	bd update <id> --claim --json
136	bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
137	```
138	
139	**Complete work:**
140	
141	```bash
142	bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
143	```
144	
145	### Issue Types
146	
147	- `bug` - Something broken
148	- `feature` - New functionality
149	- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
150	- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
151	- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
152	
153	### Priorities
154	
155	- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
156	- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
157	- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
158	- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
159	- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
160	
161	### Workflow for AI Agents
162	
163	1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
164	2. **Claim your task atomically**: `bd update <id> --claim`
165	3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
166	4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
167	   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
168	5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
169	
170	### Auto-Sync
171	
172	bd automatically syncs via Dolt:
173	
174	- Each write auto-commits to Dolt history
175	- Use `bd dolt push`/`bd dolt pull` for remote sync
176	- No manual export/import needed!
177	
178	### Important Rules
179	
180	- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
181	- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
182	- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
183	- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
184	- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
185	- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
186	- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems
187	
188	For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.
189	
190	## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
191	
192	**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
193	
194	**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
195	
196	1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
197	2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
198	3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
199	4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
200	   ```bash
201	   git pull --rebase
202	   bd dolt push
203	   git push
204	   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
205	   ```
206	5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
207	6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
208	7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
209	
210	**CRITICAL RULES:**
211	- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
212	- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
213	- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
214	- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
215	
216	<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->
217	<!-- compound-agent:start -->
218	## Compound Agent Integration
219	
220	This project uses compound-agent for session memory via **CLI commands**.
221	
222	### CLI Commands (ALWAYS USE THESE)
223	
224	**You MUST use CLI commands for lesson management:**
225	
226	| Command | Purpose |
227	|---------|---------|
228	| `ca search "query"` | Search lessons - MUST call before architectural decisions; use anytime you need context |
229	| `ca knowledge "query"` | Semantic search over project docs - MUST call before architectural decisions; use keyword phrases, not questions |
230	| `ca learn "insight"` | Capture lessons - use AFTER corrections or discoveries |
231	| `ca list` | List all stored lessons |
232	| `ca show <id>` | Show details of a specific lesson |
233	| `ca wrong <id>` | Mark a lesson as incorrect |
234	
235	### Mandatory Recall
236	
237	You MUST call `ca search` and `ca knowledge` BEFORE:
238	- Architectural decisions or complex planning
239	- Patterns you've implemented before in this repo
240	- After user corrections ("actually...", "wrong", "use X instead")
241	
242	**NEVER skip search for complex decisions.** Past mistakes will repeat.
243	
244	Beyond mandatory triggers, use these commands freely — they are lightweight queries, not heavyweight operations. Uncertain about a pattern? `ca search`. Need a detail from the docs? `ca knowledge`. The cost of an unnecessary search is near-zero; the cost of a missed one can be hours.
245	
246	### Capture Protocol
247	
248	Run `ca learn` AFTER:
249	- User corrects you
250	- Test fail -> fix -> pass cycles
251	- You discover project-specific knowledge
252	
253	**Workflow**: Search BEFORE deciding, capture AFTER learning.
254	
255	### Quality Gate
256	
257	Before capturing, verify the lesson is:
258	- **Novel** - Not already stored
259	- **Specific** - Clear guidance
260	- **Actionable** (preferred) - Obvious what to do
261	
262	### Never Edit JSONL Directly
263	
264	**WARNING: NEVER edit .claude/lessons/index.jsonl directly.**
265	
266	The JSONL file requires proper ID generation, schema validation, and SQLite sync.
267	Use CLI (`ca learn`) — never manual edits.
268	
269	See [documentation](https://github.com/Nathandela/compound-agent) for more details.
270	<!-- compound-agent:end -->

docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md-51-- **Sample rate (Fs)**: Samples per second; browser standard is 48000 Hz or 44100 Hz.
docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md-52-- **Nyquist frequency**: Fs/2; maximum representable frequency without aliasing.
docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md-53-- **Render quantum**: The atomic audio processing block in Web Audio API -- 128 samples.
docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md:54:- **AudioWorklet**: The W3C API for running custom DSP code on the audio rendering thread.
docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md-55-- **Partial**: A sinusoidal component of a complex waveform, at a harmonic or inharmonic frequency.
docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md-56-- **Aliasing**: Spectral fold-over when a signal contains energy above the Nyquist frequency.
docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md-57-- **Band-limited oscillator (BLO)**: An oscillator whose spectrum is bounded to [0, Nyquist].
--

docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md:112:The Web Audio API processes audio on a dedicated audio rendering thread separate from the main JavaScript thread. AudioWorklet processors run on this thread with a guaranteed 128-sample block period. Communication with the main thread occurs via MessagePort (asynchronous, variable latency) or AudioParam automation (sample-accurate, scheduled ahead of time).

docs/research/development/web-audio/audio-qa-methodologies.md-36-Despite the complexity of the domain, audio applications are frequently shipped with little or no automated test coverage. Several factors conspire to produce this outcome:
/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/audio-qa-methodologies.md-37-
docs/research/development/web-audio/audio-qa-methodologies.md-38-- **The oracle problem**: it is not always clear what the "correct" output of a complex audio system is. Engineers write tests when they can articulate pass/fail criteria, and perceptual audio quality resists that articulation.
docs/research/development/web-audio/audio-qa-methodologies.md:39:- **Real-time non-determinism**: the Web Audio API is specified with some intentional latitude. Thread scheduling, OS audio driver behavior, and garbage collection pauses all influence audio output in ways that make naive comparison tests flaky.
docs/research/development/web-audio/audio-qa-methodologies.md-40-- **Lack of tooling precedent**: web frontend testing tooling (Vitest, Jest, Playwright) was designed for DOM manipulation and HTTP requests, not for analyzing floating-point sample buffers.
docs/research/development/web-audio/audio-qa-methodologies.md-41-- **OfflineAudioContext underutilization**: most engineers are unaware that the Web Audio API provides a synchronous, deterministic rendering mode that eliminates real-time non-determinism from tests.

docs/research/development/web-audio/web-audio-api-architecture.md-18-Building a DAW in the browser confronts a fundamental tension: JavaScript runtimes are non-deterministic garbage-collected environments, while real-time audio demands bounded, predictable execution within render quanta measured in milliseconds. A dropped frame in a video game is a visual artifact; a dropped audio buffer produces an audible glitch that destroys the user experience.
/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/web-audio-api-architecture.md-19-
docs/research/development/web-audio/web-audio-api-architecture.md:20:The Web Audio API's design philosophy resolves this tension through strict thread separation. The _main thread_ handles UI, scheduling decisions, and parameter automation, while a high-priority _audio rendering thread_ executes the audio graph. The `AudioWorklet` API exposes the audio rendering thread to developer-defined processors, bridging the gap between the browser's JavaScript VM and the deterministic, allocation-free code style demanded by professional audio software.

/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/web-audio-api-architecture.md-22-The W3C Web Audio API Level 1 specification (CR snapshot, January 2021) defines the normative behavior [1]. /Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/web-audio-api-architecture.md-43-The audio rendering thread processes audio in fixed-size blocks called **render quanta**. The specification fixes the render quantum at **128 frames** [3]. At a sample rate of 44,100 Hz this is approximately 2.9 ms per quantum; at 48,000 Hz it is approximately 2.67 ms. The audio engine must complete all processing for one quantum before beginning the next, or a buffer underrun (glitch) occurs.

1	/**
2	 * SharedArrayBuffer byte layout for lock-free audio-thread -> UI-thread communication.
3	 * All offsets are in bytes. Float32 values use 4-byte alignment.
4	 *
5	 * INV-3: Zero-allocation convention -- all buffers pre-allocated at init,
6	 * no object/array creation in the render loop.
7	 */
8	
9	/** Byte offsets into the shared metering buffer */
10	export const MeteringLayout = {
11	  /** Master peak L (Float32) */
12	  MASTER_PEAK_L: 0,
13	  /** Master peak R (Float32) */
14	  MASTER_PEAK_R: 4,
15	  /** Master RMS L (Float32) */
16	  MASTER_RMS_L: 8,
17	  /** Master RMS R (Float32) */
18	  MASTER_RMS_R: 12,
19	  /** Per-track metering starts here. Each track: peakL, peakR, rmsL, rmsR (16 bytes) */
20	  TRACKS_OFFSET: 16,
21	  /** Bytes per track metering block */
22	  BYTES_PER_TRACK: 16,
23	} as const;
24	
25	/**
26	 * Byte offsets into the shared transport/cursor buffer.
27	 *
28	 * Note on atomics: Float32/Float64 views cannot use Atomics directly.
29	 * CURSOR_SECONDS and BPM must be read/written via integer-typed views
30	 * (e.g., Int32Array for BPM, BigInt64Array for CURSOR_SECONDS) or
31	 * accept non-atomic access with appropriate fencing.
32	 */
33	export const TransportLayout = {
34	  /** Playback cursor position in seconds (Float64, 8 bytes) */
35	  CURSOR_SECONDS: 0,
36	  /** Transport state: 0=stopped, 1=playing, 2=paused (Uint8) */
37	  STATE: 8,
38	  // Bytes 9-11: alignment padding (reserved, do not use)
39	  /** Current BPM (Float32, 4-byte aligned) */
40	  BPM: 12,
41	  /** Total size of the transport buffer */
42	  TOTAL_BYTES: 16,
43	} as const;
44	
45	export type SharedArrayBufferLayout = {
46	  metering: SharedArrayBuffer;
47	  transport: SharedArrayBuffer;
48	};
49	
50	/**
51	 * Creates the shared buffers for audio-thread <-> UI-thread communication.
52	 * @param maxTracks - Maximum number of tracks supported (default 64)
53	 */
54	export function createSharedBuffers(maxTracks = 64): SharedArrayBufferLayout {
55	  const meteringSize =
56	    MeteringLayout.TRACKS_OFFSET + maxTracks * MeteringLayout.BYTES_PER_TRACK;
57	  return {
58	    metering: new SharedArrayBuffer(meteringSize),
59	    transport: new SharedArrayBuffer(TransportLayout.TOTAL_BYTES),
60	  };
61	}

1500	### 9.5 MIDI Clip Recording Pipeline
1501	
1502	```
1503	Web MIDI API input event
1504	  → timestamp correction (latency compensation)
1505	  → beat position calculation
1506	  → note-on: create NoteOn record with openNoteMap[note] = { id, startBeats, velocity }
1507	  → note-off: close note from openNoteMap, compute durationBeats, add MidiNote to clip
1508	  → real-time UI update (show notes appearing in piano roll)
1509	```
1510	
1511	The `openNoteMap` handles the case where note-on and note-off are processed in separate audio callbacks.
1512	
1513	---
1514	
1515	## 10. Comparative Synthesis
1516	
1517	### 10.1 Architectural Comparison Table
1518	
1519	| Dimension | Ableton Live 12 | Logic Pro 11 | Reaper 7 | Browser DAW (Web) |
1520	|---|---|---|---|---|
1521	| **Primary time unit** | Beats (float64) | Ticks (480 PPQN) | Samples + beats | Beats (float64) |
1522	| **Clip model** | Clip with no takes | Region (take stacks) | Item + N takes | AudioClip (see §4.1) |
1523	| **Undo model** | State snapshot delta | Command (forward/inverse) | Command (forward/inverse) | Immer snapshots |
1524	| **Routing model** | Fixed track types | Flexible aux routing | Any-to-any matrix | Graph with typed edges |
1525	| **Solo model** | SIP | SIP + Implicit | SIP + AFL/PFL | SIP (configurable) |
1526	| **Automation** | Envelope segments | Hyper Draw regions | Per-point per-segment | Breakpoint lanes |
1527	| **MIDI quantize** | Groove extraction | Logic groove templates | Script-based | Hard + groove |
1528	| **Session format** | Binary (.als = ZIP + XML) | Package directory (.logicx) | RPP (text, key-value) | JSON |
1529	| **Auto-save** | Yes (30s) | Yes (30s crash recovery) | Undo history backup | IndexedDB |
1530	| **VCA groups** | No | Yes | Yes | Implementation choice |
1531	| **Sidechain** | Post-fader only | Pre/post selectable | Any tap point | AudioWorklet custom |
1532	
1533	### 10.2 Transport System Trade-offs
1534	
1535	| Approach | Accuracy | Complexity | Flexibility |
1536	|---|---|---|---|
1537	| Fixed BPM only | Sample-exact | Low | Low (no tempo changes) |
1538	| Tempo map (constant segments) | Sample-exact | Medium | High |
1539	| Linear tempo ramps | Sub-sample error (float) | Medium-high | Very high |
1540	| Beat-clock (no absolute sample) | Beat-accurate | Low | Medium |
1541	
1542	For a browser DAW, a tempo map with constant-BPM segments is the correct starting point. Linear ramps add significant complexity and are needed only for expressive tempo automation.
1543	
1544	### 10.3 Undo/Redo Strategy Trade-offs
1545	
1546	| Strategy | Memory | Implementation complexity | Correctness risk |
1547	|---|---|---|---|
1548	| Full state snapshots | O(N * history_depth) | Low | Low |
1549	| Structural sharing (Immer) | O(delta * history_depth) | Low-medium | Low |
1550	| Forward/inverse commands | O(delta * history_depth) | High | Medium (inverse bugs) |
1551	| CRDT-based | O(operations) | Very high | Low |
1552	
1553	For a browser DAW, Immer with structural sharing is the pragmatic choice: React and TypeScript make the immutable state model natural, the overhead is acceptable for sessions up to ~10,000 clips, and the implementation is far simpler than maintaining correct inverse operations for every edit type.
1554	
1555	### 10.4 Signal Routing Graph Trade-offs
1556	
1557	| Model | Flexibility | Complexity | Cycle detection |
1558	|---|---|---|---|
1559	| Fixed track type hierarchy | Low | Low | Impossible by design |
1560	| Soft-typed routing (Reaper) | Maximum | High | Required at every connection |
1561	| Summing stacks (Logic) | Medium | Medium | Required |
1562	| Any-to-any with type checks | High | Medium-high | Required |
1563	
1564	---
1565	
1566	## 11. Open Problems and Unsolved Challenges
1567	
1568	### 11.1 Web Audio Timing Precision
1569	
1570	The Web Audio API's `AudioContext.currentTime` has been documented to have jitter on certain platforms (Android Chrome, Safari on lower-powered devices). For a production browser DAW, this requires:
1571	
1572	- Testing on all target platforms
1573	- Fallback to `performance.now()` for scheduling with appropriate fudge factor
1574	- Using `AudioWorkletProcessor` for truly sample-accurate scheduling rather than relying on `currentTime`
1575	
1576	### 11.2 Multi-Track Recording Synchronization
1577	
1578	Recording multiple tracks simultaneously requires that all input streams (from the same `MediaStream` source) are sample-aligned. The Web Audio API provides no guarantee of alignment between separate `MediaStreamAudioSourceNode` instances connected to the same `AudioContext`. This is a known limitation for browser-based multi-track recording.
1579	
1580	### 11.3 Plugin (Audio Worklet) Latency Compensation
1581	
1582	When a plugin (AudioWorkletNode) introduces lookahead processing (e.g., a look-ahead compressor), the DAW must delay all other tracks by the same amount to maintain alignment. This **plugin latency compensation (PDC)** is a complex graph-level problem:
1583	
1584	- Reaper and Logic Pro implement automatic PDC
1585	- Ableton Live has manual PDC per plugin
1586	- Web Audio has no native PDC mechanism; it must be implemented in the routing layer by inserting `DelayNode` instances with the correct delay value
1587	
1588	### 11.4 Large Session Performance
1589	
1590	At 200+ tracks with complex routing, the JavaScript audio graph management overhead can become a bottleneck. Options include:
1591	
1592	- Moving graph traversal to an `AudioWorkletProcessor` (runs off main thread)
1593	- Using SharedArrayBuffer to share state between main thread and audio worklet
1594	- Compiling the graph to a flat series of operations (like LLVM IR) for the worklet
1595	
1596	### 11.5 Offline Rendering (Bounce/Export)
1597	
1598	Web Audio's `OfflineAudioContext` enables offline rendering at faster-than-real-time. However, `AudioWorklet` processors do not run in `OfflineAudioContext` in all browsers (historically a WebKit limitation). This means custom processing that relies on AudioWorklet may not be usable for offline bounce.
1599	
1600	### 11.6 MIDI 2.0
1601	
1602	The MIDI 2.0 standard (ratified 2020) introduces:
1603	
1604	- 32-bit resolution for controllers (vs 7-bit in MIDI 1.0)
1605	- Per-note controllers
1606	- Bidirectional property exchange (MIDI-CI)
1607	
1608	Web MIDI API currently exposes MIDI 1.0 only. MIDI 2.0 browser support is not yet standardized. A DAW designed today should use 32-bit internal precision for controller data and render down to MIDI 1.0 for output.
1609	
1610	---
1611	
1612	## 12. Implementation Recommendations for Browser DAW
1613	
1614	This section distills the survey into concrete architectural recommendations for a React + TypeScript + Web Audio API DAW.
1615	
1616	### 12.1 State Architecture
1617	
1618	Use a single immutable session state object with Immer:
1619	
1620	```typescript
1621	// store.ts
1622	import { create } from 'zustand';
1623	import { immer } from 'zustand/middleware/immer';
1624	
1625	type SessionStore = {
1626	  session: Session;
1627	  past: Session[];
1628	  future: Session[];
1629	  dispatch: (command: Command<Session>) => void;
1630	  undo: () => void;
1631	  redo: () => void;
1632	};
1633	```
1634	
1635	### 12.2 Audio Engine Separation
1636	
1637	Keep the audio engine (`AudioContext`, `AudioWorklet`, all `AudioNode` instances) completely separate from React state. Use a singleton `AudioEngine` class that:
1638	
1639	- Subscribes to session state changes via a diffing subscription
1640	- Updates the audio graph in response to structural changes (track add/remove, routing changes)
1641	- Exposes a `getPlayheadBeats(audioCtx: AudioContext): number` method for cursor rendering
1642	- Communicates recording events to the React layer via a message queue, not direct state mutation
1643	
1644	### 12.3 Transport Hook
1645	
1646	```typescript
1647	// useTransport.ts
1648	function useTransport(): TransportControls {
1649	  const audioEngine = useAudioEngine();
1650	  const { session, dispatch } = useSessionStore();
1651	
1652	  return {
1653	    play: () => audioEngine.play(),
1654	    stop: () => audioEngine.stop(),
1655	    record: () => audioEngine.record(),
1656	    seek: (beats: number) => audioEngine.seek(beats),
1657	    setTempo: (bpm: number) => dispatch(new SetTempoCommand(bpm)),
1658	    playheadBeats: usePlayheadBeats(audioEngine),  // animated float64
1659	  };
1660	}
1661	```
1662	
1663	### 12.4 Clip Rendering
1664	
1665	Use `<canvas>` for the arrangement view rather than DOM elements for clips. At 1000 clips, DOM-based rendering produces layout thrashing and paint overhead that the canvas approach avoids entirely.
1666	
1667	For waveform rendering, pre-compute peak/RMS data at multiple resolution levels (peak pyramids) during audio file import:
1668	
1669	```typescript
1670	interface WaveformData {
1671	  sampleRate: number;
1672	  levels: WaveformLevel[];  // level[0] = 1 sample/pixel at 1:1 zoom, level[n] = 2^n samples/pixel
1673	}
1674	
1675	interface WaveformLevel {
1676	  peaks: Float32Array;   // max abs sample per pixel-bucket
1677	  rms: Float32Array;     // RMS per pixel-bucket
1678	}
1679	```
1680	
1681	This approach (used by all three DAWs) enables O(1) waveform rendering at any zoom level.
1682	
1683	---
1684	
1685	## 13. Conclusion
1686	
1687	DAW architecture represents a mature set of engineering patterns accumulated over four decades of commercial development. The core patterns — tempo maps for beat/sample conversion, the command pattern for invertible edits, the audio graph for signal routing, and immutable session data for serialization — are well-understood and have clear implementations in TypeScript and the Web Audio API.
1688	
1689	The primary challenges for browser-based DAWs are not algorithmic but environmental: Web Audio timing jitter, multi-track recording synchronization, plugin delay compensation, and offline rendering coverage. Each has known workarounds (AudioWorkletProcessor scheduling, OfflineAudioContext with careful plugin selection, manual PDC configuration) that are acceptable for an initial implementation.
1690	
1691	The reference architecture synthesized in this survey — Immer-based immutable session state, command pattern undo/redo, AudioWorklet-based render engine, canvas arrangement view with peak pyramids, and JSON session format with relative media pool paths — is the pragmatic path to a production-quality browser DAW.
1692	
1693	---

1242	
1243	**OfflineAudioContext limitations for large projects**:
1244	
1245	1. The entire rendered buffer must fit in memory. At 48,000 Hz, stereo, 32-bit float, 1 hour of audio requires ~1.4 GB of RAM.
1246	2. The audio graph must be fully reconstructed—nodes cannot be shared between a live `AudioContext` and an `OfflineAudioContext`.
1247	3. There is no standard mechanism for progress reporting during rendering; the Web Audio API v2 issue tracker documents proposals for progressive/chunked rendering [14].
1248	
1249	For large projects, the practical recommendation is to use the offline context for short sections (individual clips, effects renders) and assemble the final mix server-side using FFmpeg or a native tool.
1250	
1251	### 11.4 Offline Rendering with AudioWorklet
1252	
1253	`OfflineAudioContext` supports `AudioWorkletNode` from Chrome 82+. Worklet modules must be re-added to the offline context:
1254	
1255	```typescript
1256	async function offlineWithWorklet(durationSeconds: number): Promise<AudioBuffer> {
1257	  const offlineCtx = new OfflineAudioContext({
1258	    numberOfChannels: 2,
1259	    length: Math.ceil(durationSeconds * 48000),
1260	    sampleRate: 48000,
1261	  });
1262	
1263	  // Re-register worklet modules in the offline context
1264	  await offlineCtx.audioWorklet.addModule("/worklets/compressor-processor.js");
1265	
1266	  const compressor = new AudioWorkletNode(offlineCtx, "compressor-processor");
1267	  const source = offlineCtx.createBufferSource();
1268	  // ... configure source ...
1269	
1270	  source.connect(compressor).connect(offlineCtx.destination);
1271	  source.start(0);
1272	
1273	  return offlineCtx.startRendering();
1274	}
1275	```
1276	
1277	---
1278	
1279	## 12. Comparative Synthesis
1280	
1281	| Concern | Approach A | Approach B | Trade-off |
1282	|---|---|---|---|
1283	| Custom DSP | `AudioWorkletProcessor` (JS) | WASM in `AudioWorkletProcessor` | JS: faster iteration; WASM: no GC pauses, better perf |
1284	| Cross-thread data | `MessagePort.postMessage` | `SharedArrayBuffer` + ring buffer | MessagePort: simple, high-latency; SAB: complex, low-latency |
1285	| Scheduling | Pure `setTimeout` look-ahead | `setTimeout` + `AudioParam` automation | Automation: sample-accurate; manual scheduling: flexible |
1286	| Multiple effects | Inline `AudioWorkletNode` chain | Web Worker WASM + ring buffer | Inline: simple; Worker: scalable, isolated |
1287	| Bounce/export | `OfflineAudioContext` | Server-side render | Offline: in-browser, memory-limited; Server: unlimited, network round-trip |
1288	| Node lifecycle | Fire-and-forget (`BufferSourceNode`) | Pooled reusable nodes | Source nodes: intended to be one-shot; processors: pool to reduce GC |
1289	| Latency target | `latencyHint: "interactive"` | `latencyHint: "playback"` | Interactive: lowest latency, higher CPU; Playback: power-efficient |
1290	| Parameter changes | `.value = x` | `setTargetAtTime` / ramp methods | Direct: zipper noise; Ramp: smooth, sample-accurate |
1291	
1292	---
1293	
1294	## 13. Open Problems and Current Limitations
1295	
1296	**Fixed 128-frame quantum**: The render quantum cannot be changed. Algorithms requiring larger FFT windows (e.g., convolution reverb, spectral processing) must buffer internally across multiple quanta, adding latency. Web Audio API v2 issue #1503 proposes configurable block sizes [15].
1297	
1298	**No transport synchronization API**: There is no standard mechanism to synchronize an `AudioContext` with MIDI clock, video timecode, or a remote server clock. Developers must implement custom synchronization using `currentTime` deltas, `performance.now()`, and network time protocols.
1299	
1300	**Limited MIDI integration**: The Web MIDI API (`requestMIDIAccess`) provides raw MIDI I/O but has poor cross-browser support (Firefox requires an extension; Safari has no support as of early 2026). MIDI message timestamps are not sample-accurate.
1301	
1302	**`OfflineAudioContext` memory constraints**: No chunked rendering API exists in Level 1. Projects exceeding ~10 minutes at high sample rates risk OOM failures. The v2 spec tracks this under issue #6 [16].
1303	
1304	**`performance.now()` absence in worklet scope**: `AudioWorkletGlobalScope` does not expose `performance.now()`, making it impossible to measure DSP execution time from within the processor. This is a Level 2 deliverable but not yet shipped as of 2026 [2].
1305	
1306	**iOS COOP/COEP restrictions**: WKWebView (the engine mandated for all iOS browsers) imposes additional restrictions on COOP/COEP header support, making `SharedArrayBuffer` unreliable in iOS web apps (PWAs, Capacitor, Cordova). Ring buffer architectures that depend on SAB must provide a `MessagePort`-based fallback for iOS.
1307	
1308	**Spectre mitigations and timer precision**: Cross-origin isolation requirements for SAB were introduced as a Spectre mitigation in 2021. While the headers are now well-understood, they interact with CDN configurations, CORS policies, and third-party embedded content in non-obvious ways.
1309	
1310	---
1311	
1312	## 14. Conclusion
1313	
1314	The Web Audio API's architecture—a directed audio graph, a real-time audio rendering thread, `AudioWorklet` for developer-defined DSP, and the `AudioContext` as the root timing authority—provides a principled substrate for browser-based DAW development. The specification's threading model eliminates the main-thread audio glitch problem that plagued `ScriptProcessorNode`, and the combination of `AudioWorklet`, `SharedArrayBuffer`, and WebAssembly enables near-native DSP performance in the browser.
1315	
1316	The primary remaining gaps relative to native audio development are: (1) fixed render quantum size; (2) absence of ASIO support on Windows limiting achievable latency; (3) no standard transport synchronization protocol; and (4) iOS `SharedArrayBuffer` restrictions complicating lock-free architectures on mobile. Ongoing W3C Audio Working Group activity and the Level 2 specification are addressing several of these issues.
1317	
1318	For production DAW implementation, the recommended architecture is: AudioEngine singleton per application, look-ahead scheduler driving `AudioContext`-timed events, `AudioWorkletNode` with WASM for all DSP above trivial complexity, SPSC ring buffers over `SharedArrayBuffer` for high-bandwidth data paths, pooled reusable nodes for polyphonic voices, and `OfflineAudioContext` for per-clip bounce with server-side assembly for full-project export.
1319	
1320	---
1321

728-### 4.3.3 Modulation Targets
729-
730:Envelope-to-pitch (vibrato-on-attack, hard sync pitch shapes), envelope-to-filter-cutoff (classic "wah" sweep), and envelope-to-amplifier (VCA) are the three canonical routing targets. The modulation matrix (section 4.5) formalizes this routing.
731-
732-### 4.3.4 Re-trigger vs Legato
733-
734:In re-trigger mode, a new note-on resets the envelope to zero (or to a minimum level) and begins the attack again. In legato mode, the envelope continues from its current level, preserving the decay/sustain position. This matters for smooth melodic lines:
735-
736-```typescript
--
840-### 4.5.2 Velocity and Aftertouch
841-
842:Velocity (0-127 MIDI) maps to amplitude, filter cutoff, envelope attack time, and many other destinations. It is a per-note fixed value from note-on until note-off.
843-
844-Aftertouch (channel pressure or per-note polyphonic pressure) is a real-time continuous signal. Per-note aftertouch enables expressive MPE (MIDI Polyphonic Expression) performance where each finger controls its own pitch bend, pressure, and slide independently.
--
855-### 4.5.3 Per-Voice vs Global Modulation
856-
857:**Per-voice:** Velocity, per-note aftertouch, individual envelope outputs. Each voice carries its own copy of these values, allowing polyphonic expression.
858-
859-**Global:** Master LFOs, tempo-sync, global pitch bend, mod wheel. Applied identically to all active voices. Some synthesizers provide both global LFOs (shared phase) and per-voice LFOs (each voice has its own LFO phase, sometimes randomized at note-on for "ensemble" character).

867-The Roland TR-808 (1980) generates all drum sounds through analog synthesis -- no samples. Each instrument channel is a distinct analog circuit with fixed structure but voltage-controlled parameters (pitch, decay, accent). The step sequencer triggers these circuits.

Based on my comprehensive reading of the documents, I can now provide you with a detailed summary. Let me compile all the key information:

## Comprehensive Summary of Key Information

### PROJECT NAVIGATION (AGENTS.md)

**Project Structure:**
- **Stack**: React 19 + TypeScript 5.8 (strict), Vite 8, Vitest 4, Husky pre-commit hooks, GitHub Actions CI
- **Key Paths**: `src/audio/` (Web Audio API engine), `src/ui/` (React components), `src/state/` (state management), `docs/research/` (deep research)
- **Issue Tracking**: Uses `bd` (beads) for ALL task tracking—no markdown TODOs
- **Session Memory**: `ca search` before decisions, `ca learn` after discoveries (Compound Agent integration)
- **Quality Gates**: Tests, linters, builds required before push; non-interactive shell commands mandatory (use `-f` flags)

---

## 1. SYNTHESIZER DSP FUNDAMENTALS

### PolyBLEP (Polynomial Bandlimited Step)

**Key Points:**
- Corrects discontinuities in sawtooth/square waveforms by adding a polynomial correction term near transition points
- Computed analytically from fractional position within sample interval (O(1) per sample)
- **Strengths**: No lookup tables needed, pitch-agile, minimal latency (~5 ns per sample)
- **Limitations**: 1st-order correction leaves residual aliasing above ~Fs/6; 2nd-order PolyBLEP2 significantly improves this
- **Production recommendation**: PolyBLEP is "good quality" (~5 ns per voice), suitable for most waveforms up to ~8kHz fundamental at 48kHz

**Implementation Quality Comparison:**
| Algorithm | Anti-alias | CPU/voice | Memory | Pitch-agile | Complexity |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|-------------|------------|
| Naive | None | ~1 ns | None | Yes | Trivial |
| **PolyBLEP** | **Good** | **~5 ns** | **None** | **Yes** | **Low** |
| MinBLEP | Excellent | ~20 ns | 1-4 KB | Yes | Medium |
| Additive | Perfect | O(K) sines | None | Yes | Medium |
| Wavetable | Good* | ~10 ns | 100 KB+ | Limited | Low-Med |

**Strategic Recommendation**: "PolyBLEP provides a pragmatic balance of quality and simplicity for most waveforms; wavetable synthesis provides the highest flexibility for complex timbres; additive synthesis is reserved for scenarios where spectral precision is paramount."

---

### Voice Allocation & Polyphony

**Architecture:**
- Maintains a fixed pool of N voice objects
- On note-on: assign voice to play new note
- On note-off: transition voice to release phase, eventually becomes available again

**Voice Allocation Algorithms:**
```typescript
// Last-note priority (most common for synthesizers)
function allocateVoice(midiNote: number, velocity: number): Voice {
  // Scan pool for first IDLE voice
  let target = pool.find(v => v.state === 'IDLE');
  if (!target) {
    // Steal voice with oldest release start time (lowest priority, already dying)
    target = pool.reduce((a, b) => 
      (a.releasedAt || Infinity) < (b.releasedAt || Infinity) ? a : b
    );
  }
  target.trigger(midiNote, velocity);
  return target;
}
```

**Monophonic Modes:**
- **Retrigger mode**: New note-on resets envelope to zero, begins attack again
- **Legato mode**: Envelope continues from current level; pitch changes smoothly (essential for lead instruments)
- **Last-note priority**: Most recently pressed key takes priority; releasing it falls back to previously held key if still held

**CPU Budget & Voice Count:**
- Each AudioWorklet voice processes 128 samples/quantum (2.67 ms at 48kHz)
- Simple oscillator + filter + envelope: ~0.05-0.2 ms per voice
- Complex voices (multiple oscillators, unison, effects): 0.5-2 ms per voice
- With N=8 voices + safety margin: ~1 ms per quantum total
- **Practical limit**: 8-32 voices for complex synthesizers, 64+ for simple voices

**Voice Pooling (Critical Pattern):**
Pre-allocate fixed number of AudioWorkletNode instances; send `noteOn` messages via MessagePort rather than creating/destroying nodes dynamically. Processor outputs silence when idle, allowing "connected graph trimming" optimization in Web Audio API.

---

### ADSR Envelopes

**Architecture (4-Stage State Machine):**
```
Idle (0.0) → Note-on 
  ↓
Attack (0 → 1.0 over attackTime)
  ↓
Decay (1.0 → sustainLevel over decayTime)
  ↓
Sustain (hold at sustainLevel)
  ↓
Note-off → Release (sustainLevel → 0.0 over releaseTime)
  ↓
Idle (0.0)
```

**Curve Control:**
- `curveFactor` (4-10) controls envelope shape: 0 = linear, higher = more exponential
- Exponential approach: target value approached asymptotically rather than linearly
- Allows realistic modeling of analog circuit behavior

**Critical Design Decision:**
Custom AudioWorklet processors implement their own envelope state machines RATHER than relying on AudioParam methods (`setValueAtTime`, `setTargetAtTime`), because:
- Web Audio API AudioParam methods have quantization to render quantum boundaries (128 samples ≈ 2.67 ms)
- This is insufficient for synthesis-grade envelope control
- Custom sample-level state machines provide deterministic, predictable behavior

**Triggering Modes:**
- **Re-trigger**: Reset envelope to zero on new note-on (staccato, articulated playing)
- **Legato**: Continue from current level on new note-on (smooth, connected melody)

**Multi-Stage Extensions:**
- DAHD (Delay-Attack-Hold-Decay), AHDSR, DX7-style 8-stage: generalize ADSR with arbitrary sequences of (target, time, curve) segments

---

### LFO Implementation

**Standard Architecture:**
- Independent oscillator running continuously (or per-voice with randomized phase)
- Typical rate: 0.1-20 Hz
- Modulation destinations: amplitude (tremolo), pitch (vibrato), filter cutoff (wah), pan
- Output: normalized to [-1, 1] range, scaled by modulation depth

**Modulation Targets:**
1. **Per-voice**: Velocity, per-note aftertouch, individual envelope outputs
2. **Global**: Master LFOs, tempo-sync, global pitch bend, mod wheel (applied identically to all voices)
3. **Shared phase (ensemble)** vs **independent phase per voice** (richer texture for unison/chorus)

**Modulation Matrix Pattern:**
```
Source (Velocity, Envelope, LFO, Pitch Bend, Aftertouch, etc.)
  ↓
Amount (multiply by depth, -12 dB to +12 dB range typical)
  ↓
Destination (Pitch, Filter Cutoff, VCA level, Waveform Mix, etc.)
```

Computed once per audio block with all modulation sources evaluated at block start; no per-sample branching overhead.

---

## 2. WEB AUDIO API ARCHITECTURE

### Critical Timing Constraints

**The Render Quantum (Most Important Architectural Fact):**
- Fixed 128-sample atomic processing block (cannot be changed)
- At 48 kHz: 128/48000 = 2.67 ms per quantum
- Audio engine MUST complete all processing before next quantum or buffer underrun (glitch) occurs
- **Every allocation, lock, or blocking operation inside `process()` risks a glitch**

**Thread Model:**
- **Main thread**: UI, scheduling decisions, parameter automation, receives recording events
- **Audio rendering thread**: Runs `AudioWorkletProcessor.process()` with high priority, isolated from main-thread jitter
- **AudioWorklet replaced ScriptProcessorNode** (which ran on main thread and caused glitches)

---

### AudioWorklet Patterns

**Memory Management - Zero-Allocation Constraint:**

The golden rule for AudioWorklet `process()`:
```typescript
// ❌ WRONG - creates allocation pressure
process(inputs, outputs, params): boolean {
  const buffer = new Float32Array(128);  // ← Allocates, triggers GC
  // ...
}

// ✅ RIGHT - pre-allocate in constructor
constructor() {
  this.workingBuffer = new Float32Array(128);  // ← Constructor only
}
process(inputs, outputs, params): boolean {
  this.workingBuffer.fill(0);  // ← Reuse, no allocation
  // ...
}
```

**Key Constraints:**
1. **No allocation inside `process()`** - Pre-allocate all working buffers in constructor
2. **Avoid closures and string operations** inside `process()` - Can trigger allocations
3. **Use typed arrays exclusively** - `Float32Array` operations are JIT-optimized, no boxing
4. **No object creation** - Reuse pre-allocated pools

**Preventing Garbage Collection Pauses:**
- GC pauses on main thread are acceptable; audio thread pauses are audible glitches
- JavaScript garbage collection is non-deterministic; avoid triggering it inside audio processing
- Structured memory pools allow deterministic, bounded behavior

---

### Look-Ahead Scheduler Pattern

**Fundamental Rule (Chris Wilson - "A Tale of Two Clocks"):**
- **NEVER use `setTimeout`/`setInterval` directly to trigger audio events**
- Timer queue can be delayed by GC, layout reflows, busy event loops
- Instead: Use timers only to **schedule** events ahead of time using `AudioContext.currentTime` as target

**Scheduler Architecture:**
```
Main thread periodic timer (every ~100 ms)
  ↓
Check if any events in queue fall within lookahead window
  ↓
Schedule AudioParam automation or send MessagePort message with 
  targetTime = audioCtx.currentTime + X (where X > lookahead window)
  ↓
Audio thread at exact targetTime executes the event
```

**Lookahead time**: Typically 100-500 ms ahead of current playback position. Prevents timing jitter from quantization to audio blocks.

---

### Web MIDI Integration & Timing

**Challenges:**
- Web MIDI API provides raw MIDI I/O, poor cross-browser support
- MIDI message timestamps are NOT sample-accurate
- Android Chrome and Safari have documented `AudioContext.currentTime` jitter
- Web MIDI API currently exposes MIDI 1.0 only (no MIDI 2.0 support as of early 2026)

**Solution Pattern:**
- Timestamp correction: Apply latency compensation at the input boundary
- Beat position calculation: Map MIDI timestamps to Beats using tempo map
- Use MessagePort for sample-accurate triggering with scheduled startTime

---

### Offline Rendering (OfflineAudioContext)

**Use Cases:**
- Bounce individual clips, not full projects
- Synchronous, deterministic rendering (eliminates real-time non-determinism)
- Full-project export should use server-side rendering (FFmpeg, native tool)

**Limitations:**
1. Entire rendered buffer must fit in memory (~1.4 GB for 1 hour stereo at 48kHz 32-bit float)
2. Audio graph must be fully reconstructed (nodes not shared with live AudioContext)
3. No standard progress reporting mechanism
4. AudioWorklet support varies by browser (requires module re-add per offline context)

---

## 3. DAW DESIGN PATTERNS

### State Architecture

**Recommended Approach: Immutable Session State with Immer**
```typescript
interface Session {
  bpm: number;
  timeSignature: [number, number];
  tracks: AudioTrack[];
  clips: AudioClip[];
  routingGraph: RoutingEdge[];
  // ... other properties
}

// Store with Immer structural sharing
const useSessionStore = create<SessionStore>(
  immer((set) => ({
    session: initialSession,
    past: [],
    future: [],
    dispatch: (command) => set(state => applyCommand(state, command))
  }))
);
```

**Why Immer?**
- React/TypeScript make immutable model natural
- Structural sharing keeps memory overhead acceptable (~1-10 MB per undo level)
- Implementation complexity is LOW
- Simpler than command/inverse pattern (lower correctness risk)

---

### Transport & Timing

**Tempo Map (Sample-Exact Accuracy):**
```typescript
interface TempoSegment {
  startBeats: number;
  startSamples: number;
  bpm: number;
}

// Query: what sample corresponds to beat position?
function beatsToSamples(beats: number, sampleRate: number): number {
  const segment = findSegment(beats);
  const offset = beats - segment.startBeats;
  const offsetSamples = (offset / (segment.bpm / 60)) * sampleRate;
  return segment.startSamples + offsetSamples;
}
```

**Why Tempo Maps?**
- Sample-exact accuracy for all timing operations
- Supports arbitrary tempo changes (constant-BPM segments)
- Linear ramps add significant complexity (not recommended for initial implementation)

---

### Undo/Redo Strategy

**Comparison:**

| Strategy | Memory | Complexity | Correctness Risk |
|----------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Full snapshots | O(N × depth) | Low | Low |
| Immer (structural sharing) | O(delta × depth) | **Low-medium** | **Low** |
| Forward/inverse commands | O(delta × depth) | High | Medium |
| CRDT-based | O(operations) | Very high | Low |

**Recommendation**: Immer structural sharing
- Acceptable memory overhead for sessions up to ~10,000 clips
- Far simpler than maintaining correct inverse operations for every edit type
- Plays naturally with React's immutable architecture

---

### Signal Routing Graph

**Architecture:**
- Nodes: Tracks, auxiliary sends, master bus
- Edges: Typed connections (audio, sidechain, parameter mod)
- Cycle detection required: check before every connection
- **Prevent feedback loops**: Traverse graph to ensure no path returns to source

**Pattern:**
```typescript
type RoutingEdge = {
  fromNode: string;  // source track/aux/master
  toNode: string;    // destination track/aux/master
  type: 'audio' | 'sidechain' | 'modulation';
  gain?: number;
};

function canConnect(from: string, to: string): boolean {
  return !hasCyclePath(from, to);  // DFS/BFS cycle check
}
```

---

### MIDI Recording Pipeline

**Pipeline:**
```
Web MIDI API input event
  ↓ timestamp correction (latency compensation)
  ↓ beat position calculation (map to Beats via tempo map)
  ↓ note-on: create NoteOn record with openNoteMap[note] = { id, startBeats, velocity }
  ↓ note-off: close note from openNoteMap, compute durationBeats, add MidiNote to clip
  ↓ real-time UI update (show notes appearing in piano roll)
```

**Key Detail**: `openNoteMap` handles case where note-on and note-off are processed in separate audio callbacks.

---

## 4. IMPLEMENTATION RECOMMENDATIONS

### Oscillator Strategy
- **PolyBLEP**: Default choice for most waveforms (pragmatic quality/simplicity balance)
- **Wavetable**: Highest flexibility for complex/morphing timbres
- **Additive**: Reserved for scenarios requiring spectral precision

### Filter Strategy
- **ZDF (Zero-Delay Feedback) State-Variable**: Default for synthesis-grade frequency sweeping; stable, alias-free
- **Moog Ladder Model**: For nonlinear warmth (analog synthesis character)
- **Native BiquadFilterNode**: EQ and static spectral shaping only

### Modulation Architecture
- **Clean source-amount-destination matrix**
- Evaluated once per audio block
- All modulation sources computed at block start
- No per-sample branching overhead

---

## 5. OPEN PROBLEMS & GAPS (As of Early 2026)

1. **AudioWorklet WASM Sharing**: No standardized way to share single WASM instance across multiple AudioWorkletNode instances
2. **AudioParam Scheduling Precision**: Quantized to render quantum (128 samples ≈ 2.67 ms); custom AudioWorklet envelopes required for better precision
3. **Cross-Browser Render Quantum**: Historically varied (Safari was non-standard); now converged on 128 samples
4. **Oversampling for Nonlinear Processing**: No native browser mechanism except `WaveShaperNode.oversample` (2x, 4x only)
5. **SIMD in AudioWorklet**: Available only in WASM, not JavaScript; creates development friction
6. **Phase Continuity in Voice Stealing**: No standard solution; implementations vary (hard steal, fade steal, phase-match steal)
7. **iOS SharedArrayBuffer Restrictions**: COOP/COEP headers + WKWebView limitations require MessagePort fallback
8. **Offline Rendering Coverage**: OfflineAudioContext doesn't support AudioWorklet in all browsers; full-project export should use server-side rendering
9. **Web Audio Timing Jitter**: Documented on Android Chrome and Safari; requires platform testing and fallback strategies
10. **Plugin Latency Compensation (PDC)**: No native mechanism; must implement manually by inserting DelayNode instances

---

## File Locations for Reference

- **Synthesizer DSP Fundamentals**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md`

content.text:
Based on my comprehensive reading of the documents, I can now provide you with a detailed summary. Let me compile all the key information:

## Comprehensive Summary of Key Information

### PROJECT NAVIGATION (AGENTS.md)

**Project Structure:**
- **Stack**: React 19 + TypeScript 5.8 (strict), Vite 8, Vitest 4, Husky pre-commit hooks, GitHub Actions CI
- **Key Paths**: `src/audio/` (Web Audio API engine), `src/ui/` (React components), `src/state/` (state management), `docs/research/` (deep research)
- **Issue Tracking**: Uses `bd` (beads) for ALL task tracking—no markdown TODOs
- **Session Memory**: `ca search` before decisions, `ca learn` after discoveries (Compound Agent integration)
- **Quality Gates**: Tests, linters, builds required before push; non-interactive shell commands mandatory (use `-f` flags)

---

## 1. SYNTHESIZER DSP FUNDAMENTALS

### PolyBLEP (Polynomial Bandlimited Step)

**Key Points:**
- Corrects discontinuities in sawtooth/square waveforms by adding a polynomial correction term near transition points
- Computed analytically from fractional position within sample interval (O(1) per sample)
- **Strengths**: No lookup tables needed, pitch-agile, minimal latency (~5 ns per sample)
- **Limitations**: 1st-order correction leaves residual aliasing above ~Fs/6; 2nd-order PolyBLEP2 significantly improves this
- **Production recommendation**: PolyBLEP is "good quality" (~5 ns per voice), suitable for most waveforms up to ~8kHz fundamental at 48kHz

**Implementation Quality Comparison:**
| Algorithm | Anti-alias | CPU/voice | Memory | Pitch-agile | Complexity |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|--------|-------------|------------|
| Naive | None | ~1 ns | None | Yes | Trivial |
| **PolyBLEP** | **Good** | **~5 ns** | **None** | **Yes** | **Low** |
| MinBLEP | Excellent | ~20 ns | 1-4 KB | Yes | Medium |
| Additive | Perfect | O(K) sines | None | Yes | Medium |
| Wavetable | Good* | ~10 ns | 100 KB+ | Limited | Low-Med |

**Strategic Recommendation**: "PolyBLEP provides a pragmatic balance of quality and simplicity for most waveforms; wavetable synthesis provides the highest flexibility for complex timbres; additive synthesis is reserved for scenarios where spectral precision is paramount."

---

### Voice Allocation & Polyphony

**Architecture:**
- Maintains a fixed pool of N voice objects
- On note-on: assign voice to play new note
- On note-off: transition voice to release phase, eventually becomes available again

**Voice Allocation Algorithms:**
```typescript
// Last-note priority (most common for synthesizers)
function allocateVoice(midiNote: number, velocity: number): Voice {
  // Scan pool for first IDLE voice
  let target = pool.find(v => v.state === 'IDLE');
  if (!target) {
    // Steal voice with oldest release start time (lowest priority, already dying)
    target = pool.reduce((a, b) => 
      (a.releasedAt || Infinity) < (b.releasedAt || Infinity) ? a : b
    );
  }
  target.trigger(midiNote, velocity);
  return target;
}
```

**Monophonic Modes:**
- **Retrigger mode**: New note-on resets envelope to zero, begins attack again
- **Legato mode**: Envelope continues from current level; pitch changes smoothly (essential for lead instruments)
- **Last-note priority**: Most recently pressed key takes priority; releasing it falls back to previously held key if still held

**CPU Budget & Voice Count:**
- Each AudioWorklet voice processes 128 samples/quantum (2.67 ms at 48kHz)
- Simple oscillator + filter + envelope: ~0.05-0.2 ms per voice
- Complex voices (multiple oscillators, unison, effects): 0.5-2 ms per voice
- With N=8 voices + safety margin: ~1 ms per quantum total
- **Practical limit**: 8-32 voices for complex synthesizers, 64+ for simple voices

**Voice Pooling (Critical Pattern):**
Pre-allocate fixed number of AudioWorkletNode instances; send `noteOn` messages via MessagePort rather than creating/destroying nodes dynamically. Processor outputs silence when idle, allowing "connected graph trimming" optimization in Web Audio API.

---

### ADSR Envelopes

**Architecture (4-Stage State Machine):**
```
Idle (0.0) → Note-on 
  ↓
Attack (0 → 1.0 over attackTime)
  ↓
Decay (1.0 → sustainLevel over decayTime)
  ↓
Sustain (hold at sustainLevel)
  ↓
Note-off → Release (sustainLevel → 0.0 over releaseTime)
  ↓
Idle (0.0)
```

**Curve Control:**
- `curveFactor` (4-10) controls envelope shape: 0 = linear, higher = more exponential
- Exponential approach: target value approached asymptotically rather than linearly
- Allows realistic modeling of analog circuit behavior

**Critical Design Decision:**
Custom AudioWorklet processors implement their own envelope state machines RATHER than relying on AudioParam methods (`setValueAtTime`, `setTargetAtTime`), because:
- Web Audio API AudioParam methods have quantization to render quantum boundaries (128 samples ≈ 2.67 ms)
- This is insufficient for synthesis-grade envelope control
- Custom sample-level state machines provide deterministic, predictable behavior

**Triggering Modes:**
- **Re-trigger**: Reset envelope to zero on new note-on (staccato, articulated playing)
- **Legato**: Continue from current level on new note-on (smooth, connected melody)

**Multi-Stage Extensions:**
- DAHD (Delay-Attack-Hold-Decay), AHDSR, DX7-style 8-stage: generalize ADSR with arbitrary sequences of (target, time, curve) segments

---

### LFO Implementation

**Standard Architecture:**
- Independent oscillator running continuously (or per-voice with randomized phase)
- Typical rate: 0.1-20 Hz
- Modulation destinations: amplitude (tremolo), pitch (vibrato), filter cutoff (wah), pan
- Output: normalized to [-1, 1] range, scaled by modulation depth

**Modulation Targets:**
1. **Per-voice**: Velocity, per-note aftertouch, individual envelope outputs
2. **Global**: Master LFOs, tempo-sync, global pitch bend, mod wheel (applied identically to all voices)
3. **Shared phase (ensemble)** vs **independent phase per voice** (richer texture for unison/chorus)

**Modulation Matrix Pattern:**
```
Source (Velocity, Envelope, LFO, Pitch Bend, Aftertouch, etc.)
  ↓
Amount (multiply by depth, -12 dB to +12 dB range typical)
  ↓
Destination (Pitch, Filter Cutoff, VCA level, Waveform Mix, etc.)
```

Computed once per audio block with all modulation sources evaluated at block start; no per-sample branching overhead.

---

## 2. WEB AUDIO API ARCHITECTURE

### Critical Timing Constraints

**The Render Quantum (Most Important Architectural Fact):**
- Fixed 128-sample atomic processing block (cannot be changed)
- At 48 kHz: 128/48000 = 2.67 ms per quantum
- Audio engine MUST complete all processing before next quantum or buffer underrun (glitch) occurs
- **Every allocation, lock, or blocking operation inside `process()` risks a glitch**

**Thread Model:**
- **Main thread**: UI, scheduling decisions, parameter automation, receives recording events
- **Audio rendering thread**: Runs `AudioWorkletProcessor.process()` with high priority, isolated from main-thread jitter
- **AudioWorklet replaced ScriptProcessorNode** (which ran on main thread and caused glitches)

---

### AudioWorklet Patterns

**Memory Management - Zero-Allocation Constraint:**

The golden rule for AudioWorklet `process()`:
```typescript
// ❌ WRONG - creates allocation pressure
process(inputs, outputs, params): boolean {
  const buffer = new Float32Array(128);  // ← Allocates, triggers GC
  // ...
}

// ✅ RIGHT - pre-allocate in constructor
constructor() {
  this.workingBuffer = new Float32Array(128);  // ← Constructor only
}
process(inputs, outputs, params): boolean {
  this.workingBuffer.fill(0);  // ← Reuse, no allocation
  // ...
}
```

**Key Constraints:**
1. **No allocation inside `process()`** - Pre-allocate all working buffers in constructor
2. **Avoid closures and string operations** inside `process()` - Can trigger allocations
3. **Use typed arrays exclusively** - `Float32Array` operations are JIT-optimized, no boxing
4. **No object creation** - Reuse pre-allocated pools

**Preventing Garbage Collection Pauses:**
- GC pauses on main thread are acceptable; audio thread pauses are audible glitches
- JavaScript garbage collection is non-deterministic; avoid triggering it inside audio processing
- Structured memory pools allow deterministic, bounded behavior

---

### Look-Ahead Scheduler Pattern

**Fundamental Rule (Chris Wilson - "A Tale of Two Clocks"):**
- **NEVER use `setTimeout`/`setInterval` directly to trigger audio events**
- Timer queue can be delayed by GC, layout reflows, busy event loops
- Instead: Use timers only to **schedule** events ahead of time using `AudioContext.currentTime` as target

**Scheduler Architecture:**
```
Main thread periodic timer (every ~100 ms)
  ↓
Check if any events in queue fall within lookahead window
  ↓
Schedule AudioParam automation or send MessagePort message with 
  targetTime = audioCtx.currentTime + X (where X > lookahead window)
  ↓
Audio thread at exact targetTime executes the event
```

**Lookahead time**: Typically 100-500 ms ahead of current playback position. Prevents timing jitter from quantization to audio blocks.

---

### Web MIDI Integration & Timing

**Challenges:**
- Web MIDI API provides raw MIDI I/O, poor cross-browser support
- MIDI message timestamps are NOT sample-accurate
- Android Chrome and Safari have documented `AudioContext.currentTime` jitter
- Web MIDI API currently exposes MIDI 1.0 only (no MIDI 2.0 support as of early 2026)

**Solution Pattern:**
- Timestamp correction: Apply latency compensation at the input boundary
- Beat position calculation: Map MIDI timestamps to Beats using tempo map
- Use MessagePort for sample-accurate triggering with scheduled startTime

---

### Offline Rendering (OfflineAudioContext)

**Use Cases:**
- Bounce individual clips, not full projects
- Synchronous, deterministic rendering (eliminates real-time non-determinism)
- Full-project export should use server-side rendering (FFmpeg, native tool)

**Limitations:**
1. Entire rendered buffer must fit in memory (~1.4 GB for 1 hour stereo at 48kHz 32-bit float)
2. Audio graph must be fully reconstructed (nodes not shared with live AudioContext)
3. No standard progress reporting mechanism
4. AudioWorklet support varies by browser (requires module re-add per offline context)

---

## 3. DAW DESIGN PATTERNS

### State Architecture

**Recommended Approach: Immutable Session State with Immer**
```typescript
interface Session {
  bpm: number;
  timeSignature: [number, number];
  tracks: AudioTrack[];
  clips: AudioClip[];
  routingGraph: RoutingEdge[];
  // ... other properties
}

// Store with Immer structural sharing
const useSessionStore = create<SessionStore>(
  immer((set) => ({
    session: initialSession,
    past: [],
    future: [],
    dispatch: (command) => set(state => applyCommand(state, command))
  }))
);
```

**Why Immer?**
- React/TypeScript make immutable model natural
- Structural sharing keeps memory overhead acceptable (~1-10 MB per undo level)
- Implementation complexity is LOW
- Simpler than command/inverse pattern (lower correctness risk)

---

### Transport & Timing

**Tempo Map (Sample-Exact Accuracy):**
```typescript
interface TempoSegment {
  startBeats: number;
  startSamples: number;
  bpm: number;
}

// Query: what sample corresponds to beat position?
function beatsToSamples(beats: number, sampleRate: number): number {
  const segment = findSegment(beats);
  const offset = beats - segment.startBeats;
  const offsetSamples = (offset / (segment.bpm / 60)) * sampleRate;
  return segment.startSamples + offsetSamples;
}
```

**Why Tempo Maps?**
- Sample-exact accuracy for all timing operations
- Supports arbitrary tempo changes (constant-BPM segments)
- Linear ramps add significant complexity (not recommended for initial implementation)

---

### Undo/Redo Strategy

**Comparison:**

| Strategy | Memory | Complexity | Correctness Risk |
|----------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| Full snapshots | O(N × depth) | Low | Low |
| Immer (structural sharing) | O(delta × depth) | **Low-medium** | **Low** |
| Forward/inverse commands | O(delta × depth) | High | Medium |
| CRDT-based | O(operations) | Very high | Low |

**Recommendation**: Immer structural sharing
- Acceptable memory overhead for sessions up to ~10,000 clips
- Far simpler than maintaining correct inverse operations for every edit type
- Plays naturally with React's immutable architecture

---

### Signal Routing Graph

**Architecture:**
- Nodes: Tracks, auxiliary sends, master bus
- Edges: Typed connections (audio, sidechain, parameter mod)
- Cycle detection required: check before every connection
- **Prevent feedback loops**: Traverse graph to ensure no path returns to source

**Pattern:**
```typescript
type RoutingEdge = {
  fromNode: string;  // source track/aux/master
  toNode: string;    // destination track/aux/master
  type: 'audio' | 'sidechain' | 'modulation';
  gain?: number;
};

function canConnect(from: string, to: string): boolean {
  return !hasCyclePath(from, to);  // DFS/BFS cycle check
}
```

---

### MIDI Recording Pipeline

**Pipeline:**
```
Web MIDI API input event
  ↓ timestamp correction (latency compensation)
  ↓ beat position calculation (map to Beats via tempo map)
  ↓ note-on: create NoteOn record with openNoteMap[note] = { id, startBeats, velocity }
  ↓ note-off: close note from openNoteMap, compute durationBeats, add MidiNote to clip
  ↓ real-time UI update (show notes appearing in piano roll)
```

**Key Detail**: `openNoteMap` handles case where note-on and note-off are processed in separate audio callbacks.

---

## 4. IMPLEMENTATION RECOMMENDATIONS

### Oscillator Strategy
- **PolyBLEP**: Default choice for most waveforms (pragmatic quality/simplicity balance)
- **Wavetable**: Highest flexibility for complex/morphing timbres
- **Additive**: Reserved for scenarios requiring spectral precision

### Filter Strategy
- **ZDF (Zero-Delay Feedback) State-Variable**: Default for synthesis-grade frequency sweeping; stable, alias-free
- **Moog Ladder Model**: For nonlinear warmth (analog synthesis character)
- **Native BiquadFilterNode**: EQ and static spectral shaping only

### Modulation Architecture
- **Clean source-amount-destination matrix**
- Evaluated once per audio block
- All modulation sources computed at block start
- No per-sample branching overhead

---

## 5. OPEN PROBLEMS & GAPS (As of Early 2026)

1. **AudioWorklet WASM Sharing**: No standardized way to share single WASM instance across multiple AudioWorkletNode instances
2. **AudioParam Scheduling Precision**: Quantized to render quantum (128 samples ≈ 2.67 ms); custom AudioWorklet envelopes required for better precision
3. **Cross-Browser Render Quantum**: Historically varied (Safari was non-standard); now converged on 128 samples
4. **Oversampling for Nonlinear Processing**: No native browser mechanism except `WaveShaperNode.oversample` (2x, 4x only)
5. **SIMD in AudioWorklet**: Available only in WASM, not JavaScript; creates development friction
6. **Phase Continuity in Voice Stealing**: No standard solution; implementations vary (hard steal, fade steal, phase-match steal)
7. **iOS SharedArrayBuffer Restrictions**: COOP/COEP headers + WKWebView limitations require MessagePort fallback
8. **Offline Rendering Coverage**: OfflineAudioContext doesn't support AudioWorklet in all browsers; full-project export should use server-side rendering
9. **Web Audio Timing Jitter**: Documented on Android Chrome and Safari; requires platform testing and fallback strategies
10. **Plugin Latency Compensation (PDC)**: No native mechanism; must implement manually by inserting DelayNode instances

---

## File Locations for Reference

- **Synthesizer DSP Fundamentals**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/synthesizer-dsp-fundamentals.md`
- **DAW Design Patterns**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/daw-design-patterns.md`
- **Web Audio API Architecture**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/docs/research/development/web-audio/web-audio-api-architecture.md`
- **Project Navigation**: `/Users/Nathan/Documents/Code/long-running-harness/AGENTS.md`
